cardInfo.name: Literary Review
cardInfo.notes: Best paired with long-form writing, cultural commentary, and opinion pieces. The cream background and ink-dark type evoke print on quality paper stock. Dark mode shifts to a deep warm brown rather than pure black to preserve the editorial warmth throughout.
cardInfo.summary: Literary magazine-inspired editorial card - refined cultural publication

attributes.visualDNA: Print-on-paper authority: the design implies something worth reading, not something algorithmically assembled. Cream backgrounds read as coated magazine stock. Ink-dark near-black anchors every element with editorial weight. The top border rule on cards echoes the column rules of broadsheet newspapers. Serif type carries the full load — display, body, metadata — with humanist sans reserved strictly for UI chrome. The palette avoids all cool grays; every tone pulls warm toward parchment, amber, and sepia. The through-line is intellectual appetite: everything should make the reader want to keep reading.

heading.sampleText: The Return of Third Places in Urban Life

body.sampleText: How cities are rediscovering the vital importance of informal gathering spaces beyond home and work — the café, the library, the park bench.

attributes.typographySystem: A pure-serif voice — Crimson Text carries headlines and body text with the high-contrast stroke modulation of a traditional text face. Spectral handles all secondary and metadata text: bylines, categories, captions, and UI labels. Inter is admitted only for interactive chrome where legibility at micro sizes demands a clean sans. Letter-spacing runs tight on display headings (-0.02em) and generously loose on all-caps labels (0.18em). The system reads like a serious cultural publication — authoritative, never cold.
attributes.colorPalette: A librarian's palette pulled from aged paper and printer's ink. Ivory cream anchors all backgrounds — warm enough to evoke paper grain, light enough to make dense serif type comfortable to read. Near-black ink-brown (#1a1410) provides absolute foreground authority. Golden amber and warm tan serve as secondary and muted tones, evoking foxed page edges and old card catalogue tabs. No pure white, no cool gray — every neutral leans sepia. The palette is deliberately monochromatic in warmth, relying on typographic hierarchy rather than color contrast to create structure.
attributes.historicalContext: Rooted in the golden age of literary journalism — The New Yorker's precise column rules, The Paris Review's intimate authority, and the aesthetic of mid-century cultural magazines. The aesthetic crystallized when serious writing migrated online and the best publications maintained print-derived discipline: generous whitespace, unhurried typographic hierarchy, and a visual restraint that let words do the work. The top border rule on cards is a direct reference to the column rule separating editorial from advertising in broadsheet layout.
attributes.geometricLanguage: Rectangular and rule-based. Corner radius is zero — cards read as cropped pages, not digital bubbles. The dominant geometric motif is the horizontal rule: top borders on cards, dividers between sections, underlines on links. Composition is columnar and vertical, echoing print-grid discipline. Nothing is rounded beyond the circular author avatars, which provide the only organic form in an otherwise rectilinear layout. Whitespace is treated as editorial breathing room, not empty space.
attributes.materialVocabulary: Heavyweight coated stock. Cards sit on a parchment-cream field with a subtle warm shadow that implies physical lift without drama. The top-border rule on each card reads as a printer's registration mark — functional, intentional, architectural. Hover states shift brightness by 2–3% — the sensation of turning a page toward the light. No gradients, no glass morphism, no blur effects. The material language is ink on paper: flat, direct, and confident.
attributes.compositionRules: Typography-first with editorial column discipline. Article titles command hierarchy at the largest size; excerpt and metadata follow in diminishing size/weight without overlap. Category tags are set in small-caps serif above the title — the overline convention of magazine layouts. Author attribution with initials avatar anchors the bottom of every card, separating the reading invitation from the social proof layer. Engagement statistics run in a footer row in the smallest weight, always secondary to the content. The law: the writing is always the hero.
attributes.motionLanguage: Unhurried and print-derived. Hover transitions run at 150ms ease-out with no scale or translate. Page transitions are a gentle cross-fade at 200ms — the sensation of a page turn rather than a swipe. Loading states use a slow warm pulse at 2.5s ease-in-out. No bouncing, no spring physics, no overshoot. Motion acknowledges the medium without performing it.
attributes.componentVocabulary: Status pills are small-caps italic serif with uppercase tracking — three states: published (ink-dark, highest authority), draft (warm amber, work-in-progress), scheduled (deep sienna, committed but future). Author avatars are circular ink-dark badges with cream initials — intimate and personal. Category tags are uppercase serif in the primary ink-dark color without a background chip. Divider rules are 1–2px warm tan. Primary buttons fill ink-dark; secondary actions are underlined text-links. All interactive elements respect the print metaphor: nothing shouts.
attributes.contentVoice: Authoritative, curious, and unhurried. The writing assumes an intelligent reader. Titles are declarative or essayistic — never clickbait. Excerpts are full sentences that establish the argument, not teasers. Metadata appears factually: author name, date, read time, engagement counts — no gamified language, no 'trending' labels. The tone is that of a respected editor who has thought carefully before publishing. Cultural weight is carried by specificity, not superlatives.
attributes.applicationScenarios: Long-form article cards with author byline, category, excerpt, and engagement stats. Blog post and essay collections. Editorial content grids. Author profile pages. Published / draft / scheduled content management interfaces. Literary journal or magazine-style reading experiences. Cultural commentary and opinion piece layouts.
attributes.qualityStandards: Every screen should feel like a page from a magazine you'd keep and reread. Typography must never feel cramped — generous line height and breathing room between elements are non-negotiable. All serif type must render correctly at the intended weight; if a font falls back to system serif, the layout should still hold. Status pills should never overpower the title. The test: would an editor at The New Yorker find this visually credible?
attributes.designMindset: The page as object. This design is for people who read seriously — who have opinions about typography, who subscribe to literary publications, who find beauty in a well-set column of text. The design should feel earned and considered: every decision should answer 'does this serve the writing?' Restraint is the highest form of editorial judgment here. Decoration is suspect; structure is sacred. The serif typeface is not nostalgic — it is the most legible tool for long-form reading at any scale.
inspirations: The New Yorker print layout, column rules, and typographic precision
The Paris Review, intimate authority and editorial restraint
Crimson Text and Spectral typefaces, designed for screen reading at editorial sizes
Broadsheet newspaper front pages, the horizontal rule as architectural element
Mid-century literary magazines, warm paper stock and ink-rich serif hierarchies
attributes.technicalSpecs: Base font size: 17px. Body line height: 1.7. Heading line height: 1.3. Max content width: 700px for prose, 960px for card grids. Card corner radius: 0px. Top border rule: 3px solid ink-dark. Image aspect ratios: 16:9 for article heroes, 1:1 for author avatars. Responsive breakpoints: 480px (single column), 768px (two column), 1024px (three column). Minimum tap target: 44×44px. Focus rings use the ink-dark primary color.
